news_summary: Advancements in sustainable energy technologies, significant reductions in urban air pollution, streamlined renewable energy project approvals, and eco-focused educational initiatives highlight today's positive environmental progress.
news_summary_fr: Les progrès réalisés dans le domaine des technologies énergétiques durables, la réduction significative de la pollution atmosphérique urbaine, la simplification des procédures d'autorisation des projets liés aux énergies renouvelables et les initiatives éducatives axées sur l'écologie témoignent des avancées environnementales positives observées aujourd'hui.
news_summary_es: Los avances en las tecnologías de energía sostenible, las reducciones significativas de la contaminación atmosférica urbana, la agilización de los trámites de aprobación de proyectos de energías renovables y las iniciativas educativas centradas en el medio ambiente ponen de relieve los avances positivos que se están logrando hoy en día en materia medioambiental.

articles.title: Electrodeposited self-assembled molecules for perovskite photovoltaics
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 02 July 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10844-6Electrodeposited self-assembled molecules for perovskite photovoltaics
articles.content: Preventing the detachment of self-assembled molecules (SAMs) and enhancing their passivation effect on perovskites are critical challenges for improving the performance and stability of perovskite solar cells (PSCs)1–3. Electrodeposited SAMs provide a route to improve coverage uniformity and anchoring robustness on conductive substrates beyond the limitations of conventional solution processing. Here, we use potential-cycled electrodeposition to promote molecular rearrangement and re-anchoring of SAMs, resulting in a uniform and dense layer on an indium tin oxide (ITO) substrate with enhanced anchoring capability. Building on this base SAM, functional units are grown via electrochemical oxidative coupling to form tailored coupled carbazole phosphonic SAMs, yielding power conversion efficiencies of 26.8% for lab-scale solar cells and 21.3% for solar modules (65 cm2).
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant technological advancement in perovskite solar cells that improves their efficiency and stability, which has broad implications for renewable energy and climate action. The detailed description of the electrodeposition method and the achieved high power conversion efficiencies demonstrate a meaningful breakthrough with potential large-scale environmental benefits.
articles.personality_title: New method boosts efficiency and stability of perovskite solar cells
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Perovskite solar cells (PSCs) are a promising type of solar panel that can convert sunlight into electricity. However, they face problems with molecules called self-assembled molecules (SAMs) that sometimes detach or do not protect the solar cells well enough, reducing performance and stability.

**What happened** – Scientists developed a new way to attach these SAMs more firmly to a surface called indium tin oxide (ITO) using a process called electrodeposition. This method uses electric currents to help the molecules rearrange and stick better. They also added special molecules to improve the solar cells further. This new approach helped create solar cells that convert sunlight into electricity with an efficiency of 26.8% in the lab and 21.3% in larger solar modules.

**Impact** – This improvement means solar cells can work better and last longer. The new method makes the protective layer stronger and more even, which helps the solar cells produce more electricity. Higher efficiency and stability are important because they can make solar energy cheaper and more reliable.

**What's next step** – Researchers will likely try to use this electrodeposition technique on bigger solar panels and test how well it works outside the lab. If successful, this could lead to better solar panels being used in homes and businesses.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A new electrodeposition method strengthens key molecules in perovskite solar cells, boosting their efficiency and making solar energy more reliable.
articles.personality_title_fr: Une nouvelle méthode améliore l'efficacité et la stabilité des cellules solaires pérovskites
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les cellules solaires pérovskites (CSP) sont un type prometteur de panneaux solaires capables de transformer la lumière du soleil en électricité. Cependant, elles rencontrent des problèmes avec des molécules appelées molécules auto-assemblées (MAA) qui peuvent se détacher ou ne pas protéger suffisamment les cellules, réduisant leur performance et stabilité.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Des scientifiques ont développé une nouvelle méthode pour fixer ces MAA plus solidement sur une surface appelée oxyde d'indium-étain (ITO) en utilisant un procédé appelé électrodéposition. Ce procédé utilise un courant électrique pour aider les molécules à se réarranger et mieux adhérer. Ils ont aussi ajouté des molécules spéciales pour améliorer encore les cellules solaires. Cette nouvelle approche a permis de créer des cellules solaires avec une efficacité de conversion de la lumière en électricité de 26,8 % en laboratoire et de 21,3 % sur des modules solaires plus grands.

**Impact** – Cette amélioration signifie que les cellules solaires peuvent fonctionner mieux et durer plus longtemps. La nouvelle méthode rend la couche protectrice plus forte et plus uniforme, ce qui aide les cellules à produire plus d'électricité. Une meilleure efficacité et stabilité sont importantes car elles peuvent rendre l'énergie solaire moins chère et plus fiable.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs vont probablement essayer cette technique d’électrodéposition sur des panneaux solaires plus grands et tester son efficacité en conditions réelles. Si cela fonctionne, cela pourrait mener à de meilleurs panneaux solaires pour les maisons et les entreprises.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Une nouvelle méthode d’électrodéposition renforce les molécules clés dans les cellules solaires pérovskites, augmentant leur efficacité et rendant l’énergie solaire plus fiable.
articles.personality_title_es: Nuevo método mejora la eficiencia y estabilidad de las células solares de perovskita
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Las células solares de perovskita (PSC) son un tipo prometedor de paneles solares que convierten la luz solar en electricidad. Sin embargo, enfrentan problemas con moléculas llamadas moléculas autoensambladas (SAM) que a veces se despegan o no protegen bien las células, reduciendo su rendimiento y estabilidad.

**Qué pasó** – Científicos desarrollaron una nueva forma de fijar estas SAM más firmemente a una superficie llamada óxido de indio y estaño (ITO) usando un proceso llamado electrodeposición. Este método utiliza corrientes eléctricas para ayudar a que las moléculas se reorganicen y se adhieran mejor. También añadieron moléculas especiales para mejorar aún más las células solares. Este nuevo enfoque ayudó a crear células solares con una eficiencia del 26,8 % en el laboratorio y del 21,3 % en módulos solares más grandes.

**Impacto** – Esta mejora significa que las células solares pueden funcionar mejor y durar más tiempo. El nuevo método hace que la capa protectora sea más fuerte y uniforme, lo que ayuda a las células a producir más electricidad. Una mayor eficiencia y estabilidad son importantes porque pueden hacer que la energía solar sea más barata y confiable.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores probablemente intentarán usar esta técnica de electrodeposición en paneles solares más grandes y probarán qué tan bien funciona fuera del laboratorio. Si tiene éxito, podría llevar a mejores paneles solares para hogares y negocios.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Un nuevo método de electrodeposición fortalece moléculas clave en las células solares de perovskita, aumentando su eficiencia y haciendo la energía solar más confiable.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of an elegant, glowing lattice of interconnected molecules forming a dense, shimmering layer over a smooth, translucent indium tin oxide surface, with delicate electric currents symbolized by gentle flowing light weaving through and strengthening the molecular bonds, all rendered with soft natural colors and a harmonious, abstract scientific aesthetic.

articles.title: Air pollution deaths in London fall 40 percent over five years
articles.summary:
BY THE OPTIMIST DAILY EDITORIAL TEAM Deaths linked to air pollution in London fell an estimated 40 percent between 2019 and 2024, according to a new Imperial College London study. That’s the good news. The more complicated part: the same research revised the original death estimates sharply upward, because it turns out the science had […]
The post Air pollution deaths in London fall 40 percent over five years first appeared on The Optimist Daily: Making Solutions the News.
articles.content:
BY THE OPTIMIST DAILY EDITORIAL TEAM

Deaths linked to air pollution in London fell an estimated 40 percent between 2019 and 2024, according to a new Imperial College London study. That’s the good news. The more complicated part: the same research revised the original death estimates sharply upward, because it turns out the science had been underestimating how badly pollution was hurting people.

Earlier models put roughly 4,000 premature deaths down to air pollution in London in 2019. Stronger evidence linking air quality to heart disease, dementia, and diabetes has pushed that figure to between 6,400 and 8,000. By 2024, it had come down to between 3,800 and 5,100. Progress against a worse starting point than anyone calculated.

Two findings, one clear message

Dr. David Dajnak of the Imperial Environmental Research Group was direct about what the numbers show: London’s air quality has improved substantially since 2019, and pollution “remains a serious public health risk” despite that. Nitrogen dioxide fell 41 percent across the city over five years. Fine particulate pollution dropped 28 percent. Those numbers don’t happen by accident; they follow a decade of deliberate changes to how vehicles are allowed to operate in the city.

How the ULEZ changed the equation

The Ultra-Low Emission Zone launched in central London in 2019 and expanded to inner boroughs in 2021. Vehicles that don’t meet emission standards, diesel cars from before 2015 or petrol from before 2004, pay £12.50 (about $16) a day to drive in. About 97 percent of vehicles in the zone are now compliant.

In 2023, Mayor Sadiq Khan pushed the ULEZ out to cover all of London, against significant local opposition. A study for the Greater London Authority found roadside nitrogen dioxide was 27 percent lower than it would have been without the scheme. Khan called the Imperial findings “overwhelming and unarguable” evidence that bold policy had “reduced pollution, improved public health and saved lives.”

Where the problem persists

The boroughs with the highest ratios of pollution-related deaths in 2024 were Bexley, Havering, and Sutton, all in outer London and all newly inside the expanded ULEZ. The city has also put £2.7 million (about $3.4 million) into indoor air filters for 200 primary schools and grown its zero-emission bus fleet from 30 to over 3,000 in the past decade.

Professor Stephen Holgate of the Royal College of Physicians said the scale of improvement was “so encouraging,” a reminder that sustained policy can produce “real, measurable benefits.” But Jemima Hartshorn of Mums for Lungs pointed out that more than 100,000 children were still hospitalized for breathing problems in London in 2024. “Other cities and regions are still more polluted,” she said. “Londoners need more action, and so does the rest of the country.”

Did this solution stand out? Share it with a friend or support our mission by becoming an Emissary.
articles.source: The Optimist Daily: Making Solutions the News
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant 40% reduction in air pollution-related deaths in London over five years due to deliberate policy actions like the Ultra-Low Emission Zone, demonstrating measurable public health benefits and environmental improvement with broad societal impact.
articles.personality_title: London cuts air pollution deaths by 40% in five years
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Air pollution is known to cause serious health problems like heart disease and lung issues. London has worked for years to reduce pollution and protect its residents.

**What happened** – A study by Imperial College London found that deaths linked to air pollution in London dropped by about 40% from 2019 to 2024. This improvement happened even though new research showed pollution was causing more deaths than previously thought. The city reduced harmful nitrogen dioxide by 41% and tiny dangerous particles by 28%.

**Impact** – This change came after London introduced the Ultra-Low Emission Zone (ULEZ), which started in 2019 and expanded citywide by 2023. Older, more polluting vehicles now pay a fee to drive in the city, leading to cleaner air. The result is fewer deaths and healthier people. For example, London's bus fleet switched from 30 to over 3,000 zero-emission buses in 10 years.

**What's next step** – Despite progress, some outer London areas still have higher pollution risks. The city continues to invest in air filters for schools and expanding clean transport. Experts say more action is needed to keep improving air quality and protect children and adults.

**One-sentence takeaway** – London’s strong policies on vehicle emissions have cut air pollution deaths by 40%, showing that clear rules can lead to cleaner air and better health for city residents.
articles.personality_title_fr: Londres réduit de 40 % les décès liés à la pollution de l'air en cinq ans
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – La pollution de l'air cause des problèmes de santé graves comme les maladies cardiaques et respiratoires. Londres travaille depuis des années à réduire la pollution pour protéger ses habitants.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Une étude de l'Imperial College de Londres a révélé que les décès liés à la pollution de l'air ont diminué d'environ 40 % entre 2019 et 2024. Cette amélioration intervient alors que de nouvelles recherches montrent que la pollution causait plus de décès que prévu. La ville a réduit le dioxyde d'azote de 41 % et les particules fines dangereuses de 28 %.

**Impact** – Ce changement est dû à la mise en place de la Zone à Faibles Émissions (ULEZ), lancée en 2019 et étendue à toute la ville en 2023. Les véhicules anciens et polluants doivent payer pour circuler, ce qui a rendu l'air plus propre. Résultat : moins de morts et une meilleure santé. Par exemple, la flotte de bus de Londres est passée de 30 à plus de 3 000 bus zéro émission en dix ans.

**Prochaines étapes** – Malgré les progrès, certaines zones périphériques ont encore des risques élevés de pollution. La ville continue d'investir dans des filtres à air pour les écoles et dans les transports propres. Les experts soulignent qu'il faut agir davantage pour améliorer la qualité de l'air et protéger enfants et adultes.

**Une phrase clé** – Les politiques strictes de Londres sur les émissions des véhicules ont réduit de 40 % les décès liés à la pollution, prouvant que des règles claires peuvent améliorer la santé des habitants.
articles.personality_title_es: Londres reduce en un 40 % las muertes por contaminación del aire en cinco años
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La contaminación del aire causa problemas graves de salud como enfermedades del corazón y respiratorias. Londres ha trabajado durante años para reducir la contaminación y proteger a sus habitantes.

**Qué pasó** – Un estudio del Imperial College de Londres encontró que las muertes relacionadas con la contaminación del aire disminuyeron alrededor de un 40 % entre 2019 y 2024. Esta mejora ocurrió aunque nuevas investigaciones mostraron que la contaminación causaba más muertes de las que se pensaba. La ciudad redujo el dióxido de nitrógeno en un 41 % y las partículas finas peligrosas en un 28 %.

**Impacto** – Este cambio fue posible gracias a la Zona de Emisiones Ultra Bajas (ULEZ), que comenzó en 2019 y se extendió por toda la ciudad en 2023. Los vehículos antiguos y contaminantes deben pagar para circular, lo que ha limpiado el aire. El resultado es menos muertes y mejor salud. Por ejemplo, la flota de autobuses de Londres pasó de 30 a más de 3,000 autobuses sin emisiones en 10 años.

**Próximo paso** – A pesar del progreso, algunas zonas exteriores de Londres todavía tienen altos riesgos de contaminación. La ciudad sigue invirtiendo en filtros de aire para escuelas y en transporte limpio. Los expertos dicen que se necesita hacer más para mejorar la calidad del aire y proteger a niños y adultos.

**Frase clave** – Las fuertes políticas de Londres sobre emisiones de vehículos han reducido las muertes por contaminación en un 40 %, demostrando que reglas claras pueden mejorar la salud de los habitantes.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of a clean London cityscape viewed through a translucent, protective dome symbolizing the Ultra-Low Emission Zone, with stylized, simplified electric buses and modern low-emission vehicles moving peacefully on the streets below, while soft, clear blue skies and gentle rays of sunlight filter through, highlighting the contrast between the vibrant inner city and the outer boroughs just beyond the dome’s edge where faint, muted haze lingers.

articles.title: Press release - Electricity grids: MEPs back plans to accelerate energy project permit process
articles.summary: The proposal adopted on Thursday will allow faster permitting for electricity grids and renewables projects, to help cut energy prices through the domestic supply of clean energy.Committee on Industry, Research and Energy Source : © European Union, 2026 - EP
articles.content:
MEPs on the Industry, Research and Energy Committee have backed a proposal to speed up the granting of permits for renewable energy projects, electricity grids, storage facilities, and recharging stations across the European Union, as part of the broader European grids package. The draft legislation introduces shorter deadlines, a single national digital portal for all permitting steps, and a dedicated EU-wide permitting framework for electricity grid infrastructure.

In their amendments, MEPs want to broaden and strengthen the presumption of over-riding public interest for renewable energy projects and electricity grids. They want to remove the possibility for EU member states to restrict, or create exceptions to, this presumption, except in duly justified cases strictly necessary to protect formally designated cultural heritage.

MEPs also want new rules, accelerated deadlines and more transparency for grid connection procedures. Procedures must not exceed three months, and, if the deadline passes, some projects should be tacitly approved. New grid connection deadlines are introduced for installation in and outside acceleration areas (six to nine months, depending on location and technology).

MEPs also suggest raising the capacity thresholds triggering the requirement for a permit, from 100 kW to 200 kW for small-scale solar installations, energy storage and recharging stations. Similarly, no administrative permits will be needed for the installation of recharging stations with a total installed capacity of 1MW or less on artificial structures.

Sharing the benefits

The threshold for mandatory benefit sharing (the redistribution of economic benefits generated by the project to local communities hosting or affected by it) should be lowered from 10 MW to 7 MW, say MEPs. Renewable energy communities and citizen-developed projects are explicitly exempted from the obligation. MEPs emphasise that vulnerable households should receive an adequate share of benefits.

MEPs also propose the introduction of a one-month permitting deadline for heat pumps below 50 MW (three months for ground-source), with tacit approval.

Quote

“Europe is highly dependent on costly imported fossil fuels and this needs to change. I’m happy we have adopted a very ambitious agreement that will make a real difference on the ground, without sacrificing environmental standards.” said lead MEP Niels Fuglsang (S&D, Denmark). “With this text, we are speeding up the building of Europe’s electricity grids and home-grown renewables, which is urgently needed to ensure affordable energy and energy independence through the electrification of European households and industry, not to mention its critical importance in order to reach Europe’s energy and climate targets” he added.

Next steps

The legislative proposal was adopted by 57 votes in favour and 3 against, with 8 abstentions. MEPs also voted to open negotiations with Council, by 64 votes to 3, with no abstention. Interinstitutional negotiations will start under the Irish Presidency of the Council, following the Council’s adoption of its own position on the file and once the Parliament’s mandate has been confirmed by plenary.

Background

Slow and complicated permitting procedures remain one of the main factors causing delays in the development of energy projects across the EU. Electricity transmission grids take around 10 years for completion, of which permitting accounts for more than half of the time needed, while permitting of renewable energy projects can still take up to nine years depending on the member state and the technology involved, and storage installations can take from one to seven years.

On 10 December 2025, the European Commission published a proposal to accelerate permit-granting procedures, as part of the European grids package announced under the Competitiveness Compass for the Union and the Clean Industrial Deal.
articles.reasoning: The article reports on a significant legislative proposal by the European Parliament to accelerate permitting for renewable energy and electricity grid projects, which will facilitate faster deployment of clean energy infrastructure, reduce energy costs, and support energy independence and climate goals across the EU. The impact is broad, affecting millions of Europeans and the environment positively, with detailed measures and clear benefits described.
articles.personality_title: European Parliament speeds up permits for renewable energy and electricity grids
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Europe wants to use more clean energy like solar and wind, but building the necessary projects takes a long time because of slow permit approvals. These delays can last years and hold back important energy projects.

**What happened** – On Thursday, members of the European Parliament (MEPs) voted to make the permit process faster for renewable energy projects, electricity grids, storage facilities, and charging stations. They introduced shorter deadlines, a single online portal for permits, and new rules to simplify approvals. For example, small solar and storage projects now need permits only if they are bigger than 200 kW, and some permits will be automatically approved if deadlines are missed.

**Impact** – This change means renewable energy projects and electricity grids can be built more quickly, which helps lower energy prices by increasing clean energy supply. It also supports energy independence by reducing reliance on imported fossil fuels. The new rules protect important cultural sites and make sure local communities, especially vulnerable households, benefit from nearby projects.

**What's next step** – The Parliament’s decision will now be discussed with the Council of the European Union. Negotiations will start soon under Ireland’s leadership to finalize the rules. Once agreed, these faster permit processes will begin to help build more clean energy infrastructure across Europe.

**One-sentence takeaway** – European lawmakers approved faster permit rules to speed up clean energy projects and improve energy access across the continent.
articles.personality_title_fr: Le Parlement européen accélère les permis pour les énergies renouvelables et les réseaux électriques
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – L’Europe souhaite utiliser davantage d’énergies propres comme le solaire et l’éolien, mais la construction des projets nécessaires prend beaucoup de temps à cause des lenteurs dans l’obtention des permis. Ces retards peuvent durer plusieurs années et freiner les projets importants.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Jeudi, les députés européens ont voté pour accélérer le processus de délivrance des permis pour les projets d’énergies renouvelables, les réseaux électriques, les installations de stockage et les stations de recharge. Ils ont introduit des délais plus courts, un portail en ligne unique pour les demandes, et de nouvelles règles pour simplifier les approbations. Par exemple, les petits projets solaires et de stockage ont besoin de permis seulement s’ils dépassent 200 kW, et certains permis seront automatiquement approuvés si les délais ne sont pas respectés.

**Impact** – Cette mesure permet de construire plus rapidement des projets d’énergie renouvelable et des réseaux électriques, ce qui aide à réduire les prix de l’énergie grâce à une offre plus importante d’énergie propre. Elle soutient aussi l’indépendance énergétique en réduisant la dépendance aux combustibles fossiles importés. Les nouvelles règles protègent les sites culturels importants et assurent que les communautés locales, notamment les ménages vulnérables, bénéficient des projets proches.

**Prochaine étape** – La décision du Parlement sera maintenant discutée avec le Conseil de l’Union européenne. Les négociations commenceront bientôt sous la présidence irlandaise pour finaliser les règles. Une fois adoptées, ces procédures de permis plus rapides aideront à construire davantage d’infrastructures d’énergie propre en Europe.

**Conclusion en une phrase** – Les législateurs européens ont approuvé des règles pour accélérer les permis et faciliter le développement des projets d’énergie propre à travers le continent.
articles.personality_title_es: El Parlamento Europeo acelera los permisos para energías renovables y redes eléctricas
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Europa quiere usar más energía limpia como la solar y eólica, pero construir estos proyectos toma mucho tiempo debido a la lentitud en la aprobación de permisos. Estos retrasos pueden durar años y retrasar proyectos importantes.

**Qué pasó** – El jueves, los miembros del Parlamento Europeo (MEPs) votaron para acelerar el proceso de permisos para proyectos de energía renovable, redes eléctricas, almacenamiento y estaciones de recarga. Introdujeron plazos más cortos, un portal digital único para los permisos y nuevas reglas para simplificar las aprobaciones. Por ejemplo, los pequeños proyectos solares y de almacenamiento solo necesitarán permiso si superan los 200 kW, y algunos permisos se aprobarán automáticamente si se pasan los plazos.

**Impacto** – Esto significa que los proyectos de energía renovable y las redes eléctricas se podrán construir más rápido, lo que ayuda a bajar los precios de la energía al aumentar la oferta de energía limpia. También apoya la independencia energética al reducir la dependencia de combustibles fósiles importados. Las nuevas reglas protegen sitios culturales importantes y aseguran que las comunidades locales, especialmente los hogares vulnerables, reciban beneficios.

**Próximo paso** – La decisión del Parlamento se discutirá ahora con el Consejo de la Unión Europea. Las negociaciones comenzarán pronto bajo la presidencia irlandesa para finalizar las normas. Una vez acordadas, estos procesos de permisos más rápidos ayudarán a construir más infraestructura de energía limpia en Europa.

**Frase clave** – Los legisladores europeos aprobaron reglas para acelerar los permisos y facilitar los proyectos de energía limpia en todo el continente.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of a vibrant European landscape featuring interconnected stylized electricity grids and renewable energy symbols—such as solar panels, wind turbines, and electric vehicle charging stations—linked by glowing, flowing lines that represent accelerated energy project permits, all set against a soft, natural color palette with silhouettes of diverse community buildings subtly integrated to symbolize local benefit sharing and energy independence.

articles.title: Stop the Summer Reading Slide With Eco-Themed Kids’ Books
articles.summary:
Summer is a time for playing outside and enjoying the environment. At least one study...
The post Stop the Summer Reading Slide With Eco-Themed Kids’ Books appeared first on Earth911.
articles.content:
Summer is a time for playing outside and enjoying the environment. At least one study has shown that playing outside as a child is an important predictor of protecting the environment as an adult. But parents need to ensure kids keep up their reading skills, which often slide over the summer.

These books with environmental themes, sorted by reading level, will improve both your kids’ literacy and their environmental awareness. We suggest reading them in a treehouse or on a picnic blanket in the sun.

Earth911 teams up with affiliate marketing partners to help fund our Recycling Directory. If you purchase an item through one of the affiliate links in this post, we will receive a small commission.

Picture Books

by Laura Purdie Salas

A leaf can be a … shade spiller, mouth filler, tree topper, rain stopper. Find out about the many roles leaves play in this poetic exploration of leaves throughout the year. Pair it with the companion volumes A Rock Can Be … and Water Can Be … for a full nature-cycle set.

by Megan Herbert and Michael E. Mann

A little girl inherits a huge problem she didn’t ask for — and then channels strong emotions into positive action. Co-written by climate scientist Michael E. Mann, the second half explains the science of climate change in age-appropriate language and closes with a kid-friendly action plan.

by Jen Cullerton Johnson

It’s never too early for children to see examples of strong women who make the world a better place. This picture-book biography of Nobel Peace Prize winner Wangari Maathai illustrates the often-overlooked intersection between ecology and justice, which makes this example even better.

by Carole Lindstrom, illustrated by Michaela Goade

New to this list. Winner of the 2021 Caldecott Medal, the first awarded to a Native American illustrator, this lyrical, gorgeously painted book follows an Ojibwe girl who rallies her community to defend the water against a “black snake” pipeline. It introduces the youngest readers to Indigenous environmental stewardship and the idea that water is life.

Books for Younger Middle Grade

by Joanna Cole

Trust the beloved kids’ science series Magic School Bus to explain the facts of global warming in ways kids understand, and to give them ideas about how they can help. Ms. Frizzle takes the class from the Arctic to the equator to see the signs of a warming planet firsthand.

by Hannah Gold

New to this list. There are no polar bears left on Bear Island, or so April’s father tells her when his research takes them to a remote Arctic outpost. Then April spots one: hungry, lonely, and far from home. Hannah Gold’s award-winning debut (a Waterstones Children’s Book Prize and Blue Peter Book Award winner) pairs a tender friendship story with a clear-eyed look at melting sea ice, illustrated throughout by Levi Pinfold.

by S. Terrell French

The environmental movement is too often associated with white people. In Operation Redwood, a biracial boy challenges his rich relatives to look past the profit motive and protect an old-growth redwood grove on property they own.

Books for Middle-Grade Tweens

by Alan Gratz

New to this list. From the #1 New York Times bestselling author of Refugee, this fast-moving novel braids together three kids facing three climate disasters — a California wildfire, stranded polar bears in Manitoba, and a Florida hurricane — into one connected story. It won the 2023 Green Earth Young Adult Book Award and reads like a thriller, which makes it a strong pick for reluctant readers.

by Gill Lewis

Two children living in the Congo’s war zone risk everything to protect a captured baby gorilla from a life in captivity. Although not graphic, this book is intense. It addresses the impact of violence on children and wildlife and reveals the connection between the rare-earth minerals in consumer electronics and devastating destruction in Africa.

by Carl Hiaasen

While not as overtly environmentalist as the well-known Hoot, Hiaasen’s eco-adventure features tween protagonists who care about animals and appreciate the natural world more than the adults around them — here, a Florida kid who heads to Montana to find his father and ends up tangling with poachers, a spy drone, and a grizzly. His characteristic irreverent humor is on full display.

by Piers Torday

A boy who can talk to animals — but not people — fights against extinction in a world where a virus has wiped out nearly all wildlife. The first book in a gripping trilogy, it’s a natural conversation-starter about biodiversity loss and what a landscape looks like once the wild things are gone.

by Andri Snær Magnason

From poetry to nonfiction, books by Icelandic author Andri Snær Magnason are unified by environmental concern. Now available in English, his 2013 novel for tweens and teens, The Casket of Time, tells the story of Sigrun, a teenager whose TimeBox® opens too early. Her family entered the TimeBoxes to sleep out “the situation,” but now she finds herself among the few who are left awake to fix the world. Younger readers will enjoy his first children’s book, The Story of the Blue Planet.

Make the Most of Summer Reading

A few simple habits help these books do double duty — building reading stamina and environmental awareness at the same time:

Read outside. Pairing a nature story with time in a backyard, park, or trail reinforces the connection the research points to between outdoor play and lifelong environmental care. Borrow before you buy. Most of these titles are available through your local library or its e-book app, which keeps reading low-cost and low-waste. Buy the keepers your kids want to read again. Talk about the action steps. Several of these books — The Tantrum That Saved the World, Two Degrees, Old Enough-style activist stories — end with concrete things kids can do. Pick one and try it together. Pass them on. When your family outgrows a book, donate it to a school, Little Free Library, or shelter so it keeps circulating instead of heading to the recycling bin.

Editor’s Note: This article was originally published by Gemma Alexander on May 10, 2019, and was most recently updated with new titles in June 2026.
articles.reasoning: The article promotes eco-themed children's books that simultaneously improve literacy and environmental awareness, encouraging outdoor play and lifelong environmental stewardship. It highlights a broad positive impact on children's education and environmental consciousness, with detailed book recommendations and actionable advice for families, making it a substantive and meaningful story.
articles.personality_title: Eco-Themed Books Help Kids Read and Care for Nature This Summer
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Summer break is a time when many children spend more time playing outside but often read less, which can cause their reading skills to decline. Research shows that playing outside as a child can lead to caring more about the environment as an adult.

**What happened** – A list of eco-themed children’s books has been shared to help families keep kids reading during summer while also teaching them about nature and the environment. The books are organized by reading level, from picture books to middle-grade novels, and include stories about climate change, wildlife, and environmental heroes. Some books even explain science in ways kids can understand and offer ideas for how they can help.

**Impact** – These books do two important things at once: they improve children’s reading skills and increase their awareness of environmental issues. Reading outside while enjoying nature makes the lessons in the books more real and memorable. The stories also introduce kids to diverse characters and real-world problems, helping them feel connected and motivated to care for the planet.

**What's next step** – Parents and caregivers are encouraged to borrow these books from libraries or buy copies to read with their children outdoors. They can also talk about the actions suggested in the books to make learning active. When children outgrow the books, donating them helps more kids benefit from the stories and lessons.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Eco-themed books provide a fun and meaningful way for kids to keep up reading skills while learning to protect the environment during summer break.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des livres écolos aident les enfants à lire et à protéger la nature cet été
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Pendant les vacances d’été, beaucoup d’enfants jouent dehors mais lisent moins, ce qui peut faire baisser leurs compétences en lecture. Des recherches montrent que jouer dehors dans l’enfance aide à mieux protéger l’environnement à l’âge adulte.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Une liste de livres pour enfants sur le thème de l’écologie a été partagée pour aider les familles à garder les enfants dans la lecture tout en leur apprenant la nature et l’environnement. Ces livres sont classés par niveau, des albums pour les petits aux romans pour les plus grands, avec des histoires sur le changement climatique, les animaux et des héros de l’environnement. Certains expliquent la science de façon simple et proposent des idées pour agir.

**Impact** – Ces livres font deux choses importantes à la fois : ils améliorent la lecture des enfants et leur conscience des problèmes écologiques. Lire dehors en profitant de la nature rend les leçons plus vraies et mémorables. Les histoires présentent aussi des personnages divers et des problèmes réels, aidant les enfants à se sentir concernés.

**Prochaine étape** – Les parents et éducateurs sont invités à emprunter ces livres en bibliothèque ou à en acheter pour lire avec les enfants en plein air. Ils peuvent aussi discuter des actions proposées pour rendre l’apprentissage actif. Quand les enfants n’en ont plus besoin, donner les livres permet à d’autres d’en profiter.

**Une phrase clé** – Les livres écolos offrent un moyen ludique et utile pour que les enfants gardent leurs compétences en lecture tout en apprenant à protéger la planète cet été.
articles.personality_title_es: Libros ecológicos ayudan a niños a leer y cuidar la naturaleza este verano
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Durante las vacaciones de verano, muchos niños juegan afuera pero leen menos, lo que puede hacer que sus habilidades de lectura bajen. Estudios muestran que jugar afuera en la infancia ayuda a cuidar el medio ambiente cuando son adultos.

**Qué pasó** – Se compartió una lista de libros infantiles con temas ecológicos para ayudar a las familias a que los niños sigan leyendo mientras aprenden sobre la naturaleza y el medio ambiente. Los libros están organizados por nivel, desde cuentos para pequeños hasta novelas para niños más grandes, con historias sobre cambio climático, animales y héroes ambientales. Algunos explican la ciencia de forma sencilla y dan ideas para actuar.

**Impacto** – Estos libros hacen dos cosas importantes: mejoran la lectura de los niños y aumentan su conciencia sobre temas ambientales. Leer al aire libre mientras disfrutan la naturaleza hace que las lecciones sean más reales y fáciles de recordar. Las historias también presentan personajes diversos y problemas reales, ayudando a los niños a sentirse conectados.

**Próximo paso** – Se aconseja a padres y cuidadores que pidan estos libros en bibliotecas o compren copias para leer con los niños afuera. También pueden hablar sobre las acciones que sugieren los libros para hacer el aprendizaje activo. Cuando los niños ya no los necesiten, donar los libros ayuda a que otros niños los disfruten.

**Una frase clave** – Los libros ecológicos ofrecen una forma divertida y significativa para que los niños mantengan sus habilidades de lectura mientras aprenden a cuidar el medio ambiente durante el verano.
articles.image_prompt: A cozy sunlit treehouse filled with open eco-themed children’s books resting on a wooden table, surrounded by symbolic elements like a vibrant leaf transforming into a book page, a small globe cradled by gentle hands made of green vines, and a playful silhouette of a child reading under dappled light filtering through leaves, all rendered in warm, natural earth tones with a soft, painterly style.
